title: Listening Starter Mock 01 — Chronic Pain + Medication Review

questions.explanation: Patient describes pain radiating from the lower back.
questions.transcriptExcerpt: It's the lower back, mainly.

questions.explanation: Patient states pain started six months ago.
questions.transcriptExcerpt: About six months now.

questions.explanation: Symptom worsens during prolonged sitting.
questions.transcriptExcerpt: Sitting at my desk makes it much worse.

questions.explanation: Diagnosed with sciatica two years prior.
questions.transcriptExcerpt: I was told it was sciatica two years ago.

questions.explanation: Patient takes ibuprofen as required.
questions.transcriptExcerpt: Just ibuprofen when it flares up.

questions.explanation: Patient takes 400 mg per dose.
questions.transcriptExcerpt: 400 milligrams, twice a day.

questions.explanation: Patient works at a desk all day.
questions.transcriptExcerpt: I sit at a desk all day.

questions.stem: Sleep affected: wakes ____ times per night
questions.explanation: Patient wakes three times nightly due to pain.
questions.transcriptExcerpt: Three times a night, easily.

questions.explanation: Patient has tried both physiotherapy and acupuncture.
questions.transcriptExcerpt: Physio and a bit of acupuncture.

questions.explanation: GP orders an MRI scan.
questions.transcriptExcerpt: I'll send you for an MRI.

questions.explanation: Patient advised to avoid heavy lifting until results.
questions.transcriptExcerpt: Avoid any heavy lifting for now.

questions.explanation: Review booked for four weeks.
questions.transcriptExcerpt: Come back in four weeks.

questions.explanation: Patient is 62 years old.
questions.transcriptExcerpt: I'm sixty-two.

questions.explanation: Diagnosis date is ten years prior.
questions.transcriptExcerpt: Diagnosed about ten years ago now.

questions.explanation: Patient takes amlodipine daily.
questions.transcriptExcerpt: Amlodipine, every morning.

questions.explanation: Daily dose is 5 mg.
questions.transcriptExcerpt: Five milligrams a day.

questions.explanation: Patient reports ankle swelling.
questions.transcriptExcerpt: A bit of swelling around my ankles.

questions.explanation: Last BP reading was 145/85.
questions.transcriptExcerpt: It was 145 over 85.

questions.explanation: Patient advised to reduce salt.
questions.transcriptExcerpt: Try to cut down on salt.

questions.explanation: Recommendation is 30 minutes daily.
questions.transcriptExcerpt: Thirty minutes a day of walking.

questions.explanation: Pharmacist suggests ramipril as alternative.
questions.transcriptExcerpt: We might switch you to ramipril.

questions.explanation: Blood test scheduled for next Tuesday.
questions.transcriptExcerpt: Bloods next Tuesday morning.

questions.explanation: Patient raises concerns about medication cost.
questions.transcriptExcerpt: I'm worried about the cost.

questions.explanation: Follow-up appointment in six weeks.
questions.transcriptExcerpt: I'll see you again in six weeks.

questions.stem: You hear a nurse briefing a colleague. The nurse's main concern is
questions.explanation: Nurse emphasises a recent fall and asks colleague to assist with toileting.
questions.transcriptExcerpt: She fell yesterday, so please assist with all transfers.
questions.distractorExplanation: Option B mentions chart only in passing; option C is about menu, not nutrition.
optionDistractorWhy: Mentioned only as a closing reminder, not the main concern.
The 'lunch menu' line is incidental, not a clinical concern.

questions.stem: A junior doctor is told to
questions.explanation: Charge nurse advises consultation before any treatment change.
questions.transcriptExcerpt: Before you change anything, run it past Dr Khan.
questions.distractorExplanation: Option A is not requested; option B is what the junior is tempted to do but is told to wait.
optionDistractorWhy: Not mentioned anywhere — pure keyword trap from earlier context.
Exactly what the junior is told NOT to do without consultation.

questions.stem: The pharmacist is reminding a colleague that
questions.explanation: Pharmacist points to a fridge log showing temperatures outside the 2–8 °C range.
questions.transcriptExcerpt: The fridge log shows we're sitting at twelve degrees overnight.
questions.distractorExplanation: Option A is wrong because the order is on its way; option B is the speaker, not the subject.
optionDistractorWhy: Order status was actually fine — supply will arrive Friday.
The speaker IS the pharmacist; the supplier is uninvolved.

questions.stem: During a team handover, the main change is that
options: the patient's fluids have been increased
questions.correctAnswer: the patient's fluids have been increased
questions.explanation: Outgoing nurse reports IV fluid rate doubled overnight.
questions.transcriptExcerpt: We've doubled the fluids to 120 ml an hour.
questions.distractorExplanation: Discharge is planned but not yet done; treatment is being continued, not refused.
optionDistractorWhy: Discharge is mentioned as planned for tomorrow — not yet happened.
Patient is fully co-operative; no refusal mentioned.

questions.stem: A consultant tells a trainee that the priority is to
questions.explanation: Consultant insists on talking to family before further decisions.
questions.transcriptExcerpt: First thing — go and talk to the family.
questions.distractorExplanation: Discharge summary can wait until after the family conversation.
optionDistractorWhy: Scheduled, but not urgent.
Pain is already controlled per chart.

questions.stem: The infection-control briefing warns staff to
questions.explanation: Lead nurse stresses immediate isolation as primary action.
questions.transcriptExcerpt: If you suspect even one case, side room straight away.
questions.distractorExplanation: Occupational health is the secondary step; handwashing is reinforced but not the warning.
optionDistractorWhy: A secondary follow-up, not the urgent warning.
Reinforced briefly but framed as ongoing practice, not the warning.

questions.stem: The clinician's overall view of telemedicine is best described as
questions.explanation: She acknowledges benefits but lists clear conditions for success.
questions.transcriptExcerpt: It can work very well — but only if we get the basics right.
questions.distractorExplanation: She doesn't dismiss it, nor does she advocate for it without reservation.
optionDistractorWhy: Concerns are raised, but acceptance is genuine.
She avoids unqualified praise throughout.

questions.stem: She says the biggest barrier in primary care is
questions.explanation: She highlights elderly patients struggling with apps as the key blocker.
questions.transcriptExcerpt: It's our older patients who can't navigate the app — that's where we lose them.
questions.distractorExplanation: Training is mentioned as a solved problem; broadband is a minor concern.
optionDistractorWhy: Mentioned as a previous, now-resolved issue.
A secondary concern she lists later.

questions.stem: She gives the example of a diabetic patient to illustrate
questions.explanation: She praises the patient's self-titration of insulin from data she shared.
questions.transcriptExcerpt: She was adjusting her own doses confidently based on the trends.
questions.distractorExplanation: Both A and B are real themes elsewhere — but this example is about empowerment.
optionDistractorWhy: A theme of the talk but not why she names this patient.
Mentioned as the prerequisite, not the point of the story.

questions.stem: Her concern about funding models is that they
options: treat video calls as equivalent to phone calls
questions.correctAnswer: treat video calls as equivalent to phone calls
questions.explanation: She argues the tariff under-values the clinical work in a video consult.
questions.transcriptExcerpt: Lumping video and phone together is wrong — the work isn't the same.
questions.distractorExplanation: Face-to-face incentives are touched on but not her main funding concern.
optionDistractorWhy: A neighbouring concern, not the one she elaborates on.
Not mentioned at all in this section.

questions.stem: She suggests new staff should be trained primarily on
questions.explanation: She insists safeguarding cues are the hardest skill to translate to video.
questions.transcriptExcerpt: The first thing trainees miss is the safeguarding cue.
questions.distractorExplanation: Consent and documentation are mentioned but framed as quick to teach.
optionDistractorWhy: Easy to teach — she dismisses it as 'a checklist'.
Important but second-tier in her view.

questions.stem: Her closing point is that the next two years should focus on
options: expanding the service to more specialties
questions.explanation: She explicitly says scaling without quality is the bigger risk.
questions.transcriptExcerpt: Get the quality right first — scale only when we're sure.
questions.distractorExplanation: Expansion and reduction are both treated as worse outcomes if quality slips.
optionDistractorWhy: She explicitly cautions against this without quality checks.
Not her position — she defends remote work.

questions.stem: The presenter opens by stressing that falls in hospital are
options: the single biggest source of in-patient harm
questions.correctAnswer: the single biggest source of in-patient harm
questions.explanation: She cites NHS data placing falls at the top of harm categories.
questions.transcriptExcerpt: Across our trusts, falls remain the single biggest source of in-patient harm.
questions.distractorExplanation: Option A understates frequency; option C overstates preventability.
optionDistractorWhy: She explicitly contradicts the framing as 'rare'.
She later says ~30 % are unavoidable.

questions.stem: Her main criticism of current assessment tools is that they
questions.explanation: She argues over-flagging leads to fatigue and missed real cases.
questions.transcriptExcerpt: Flagging everyone flags no one — the noise drowns out the signal.
questions.distractorExplanation: Length is mentioned but framed as acceptable; history is included by most tools.
optionDistractorWhy: She says length is fine — five minutes is acceptable.
She praises tools that DO capture history.

questions.stem: She mentions the orthopaedic ward case to illustrate
options: a need for more bedside equipment
questions.explanation: The patient was deemed low-risk by one team and high-risk by another with no shared record.
questions.transcriptExcerpt: One team had her as low risk, the other as high — nobody talked.
questions.distractorExplanation: She uses the case as a cautionary tale, not a success.
optionDistractorWhy: The opposite — the case is a failure she wants to learn from.
Equipment isn't the issue she names here.

questions.stem: Her recommended intervention with the strongest evidence is
questions.explanation: She cites a meta-analysis showing 24 % reduction with structured exercise.
questions.transcriptExcerpt: A 24 percent reduction — that's structured exercise, not pills.
questions.distractorExplanation: Vitamin D is mentioned but with weaker evidence; bedrails are explicitly cautioned against.
optionDistractorWhy: She specifically says 'not pills' — vitamin D evidence is weaker.
She warns bedrails can worsen outcomes.

questions.stem: She suggests that ward managers should
options: share fall data weekly across teams
questions.correctAnswer: share fall data weekly across teams
questions.explanation: She specifically asks for weekly cross-team data sharing as the operational lever.
questions.transcriptExcerpt: Share the data weekly — across teams, not just in one ward meeting.
questions.distractorExplanation: Simulation and chart audits are recommended elsewhere but not framed as the manager's lever.
optionDistractorWhy: A useful add-on but not what she asks managers to own.
Recommended for pharmacists, not ward managers.

questions.stem: Her final message to clinicians is
options: accept that some falls are unavoidable
trust assessment scores over clinical judgement
focus on lowering the trust-wide fall rate
questions.correctAnswer: accept that some falls are unavoidable
questions.explanation: She closes by warning against blame culture for unavoidable falls.
questions.transcriptExcerpt: Some falls will happen — punishing teams for them costs more than it saves.
questions.distractorExplanation: She argues judgement should override scores when they conflict; trust-rate fixation distorts behaviour.
optionDistractorWhy: She argues the opposite — judgement over scores.
She says rate-chasing distorts behaviour.
